task.title: Vending Machine: Dispense Product
task.description: The dispenseProduct() method body is missing. Implement it to fetch a product by ID, verify it is in stock, decrement the quantity, persist the update, and return the correct change. Reject out-of-stock products with OutOfStockException and insufficient payment with InsufficientFundsException.
constraints: Throw OutOfStockException when the product is not found or quantity <= 0
Throw InsufficientFundsException when amountPaid < product price
Check stock before checking payment — fail-cheap ordering avoids charging for an unavailable product
Decrement quantity AND persist via productRepository.save(product) — an in-memory decrement alone is a lost update
Change returned must equal amountPaid - price

correctness.9-10: All paths handled: both exceptions thrown correctly in the right order (stock first, then payment), stock persisted via save(), change is amountPaid - price
correctness.7-8: Happy path + exceptions present; one minor gap (e.g. wrong exception ordering or missing save())
correctness.4-6: Basic dispense works but misses 1-2 constraints (e.g. no save, wrong exception type)
correctness.1-3: Fundamentally wrong or incomplete — missing exception handling or wrong return

efficiency.9-10: O(1) single findById + single save; candidate names the complexity and mentions concurrent-update safety
efficiency.7-8: Correct but one unnecessary extra fetch or no complexity discussion
efficiency.4-6: Works but naive (e.g. reloads all products to find by ID)
efficiency.1-3: Ignores efficiency or introduces an obvious anti-pattern

senioritySignals: Checks stock before payment — fail-cheap ordering avoids charging for an unavailable product
Calls productRepository.save(product) after decrementing — not just an in-memory update
Derives change as amountPaid - price rather than a hardcoded or unrelated value
Notices that change is returned but never reconciled against a CashRepository
Considers @Transactional or optimistic locking for the read-modify-write on quantity
commonMistakes: Decrements quantity in memory but forgets to call productRepository.save() — lost update
Checks payment before stock — charges the buyer for a product that was already out of stock
Hardcodes change or returns amountPaid instead of amountPaid - price
No atomicity around the read-modify-write of quantity under concurrent dispenses

interviewerFocusAreas.scope: Ordering of stock vs payment checks — does it matter which exception is thrown first? Walk through the case where payment arrives for an already-empty slot.
interviewerFocusAreas.probeQuestion: If you check amountPaid < price before checking stock, what bad outcome can happen for the buyer?
interviewerFocusAreas.goodAnswerIndicator: Explains that the buyer gets charged (or mentally expects to get charged) for a product that was already sold out — the correct order is stock-first

interviewerFocusAreas.scope: Does the candidate understand that an in-memory decrement without save() is a lost update — invisible at the API level but breaks inventory consistency.
interviewerFocusAreas.probeQuestion: True or False: decrementing product.quantity in Java without calling productRepository.save() will update the database.
interviewerFocusAreas.goodAnswerIndicator: False — JPA/Hibernate only persists changes when save() is called or the entity is managed inside a @Transactional context with dirty checking

interviewerFocusAreas.scope: Read-modify-write on Product.quantity when two buyers hit the same last unit simultaneously — what happens with naive findById + decrement + save?
interviewerFocusAreas.probeQuestion: What happens when two requests call dispenseProduct() for the same last unit at exactly the same time? Walk me through the race condition.
interviewerFocusAreas.goodAnswerIndicator: Identifies that both threads read quantity=1, both decrement to 0, both call save() — net result is quantity=-1 or one call silently wins; mentions @Transactional or atomic UPDATE as the fix

interviewerFocusAreas.scope: Change is computed but never reconciled against a CashRepository — what would a correct money-flow look like?
interviewerFocusAreas.probeQuestion: The method returns change, but nowhere in the codebase is the machine's cash balance updated. What's missing and how would you model exact-change availability?
interviewerFocusAreas.goodAnswerIndicator: Proposes a CashRepository (or CashFloat) that tracks available denominations; dispense should fail if machine cannot make change; mentions idempotency for retry scenarios

expectedApproaches.tradeoff: Simple and O(1); not safe under concurrent dispenses without @Transactional or version-based optimistic locking

expectedApproaches.approach: Atomic conditional UPDATE (UPDATE products SET quantity = quantity - 1 WHERE id = ? AND quantity > 0)
expectedApproaches.tradeoff: Concurrency-safe at the DB layer without application-level locking; slightly harder to read and requires custom @Query in the repository

knownEdgeCases: Product ID not found in repository
quantity == 0 exactly at purchase time (not just negative)
amountPaid exactly equals price — zero change, must still succeed
Two concurrent dispenses of the last unit in stock

scaleUpDimensions.dimension: Multiple vending machines sharing one inventory store — distributed locking or DB-level atomicity required
Exact-change availability tracked in CashRepository — dispense should fail if machine cannot make change
scaleUpDimensions.triggerCondition: candidate noticed the missing cash reconciliation

implementationChallenges.trigger: candidate did a plain read-decrement-save without @Transactional or locking
implementationChallenges.instruction: Add @Transactional to dispenseProduct() and explain what isolation level you would choose and why. Then modify the repository to use an atomic UPDATE with a WHERE quantity > 0 guard instead of a read-then-write.
implementationChallenges.acceptanceCriteria: Two simultaneous calls for the last unit result in exactly one successful dispense and one OutOfStockException, with no inventory going below 0

implementationChallenges.trigger: candidate noticed change is returned but not reconciled against machine cash
implementationChallenges.instruction: Add a CashRepository that tracks the machine's cash float. dispenseProduct() should throw an InsufficientChangeException if the machine cannot make exact change, and should deduct the dispensed change from the float on success.
implementationChallenges.acceptanceCriteria: Dispensing when the machine has no coins to make change throws InsufficientChangeException; successful dispense updates the cash float
